race.name: Levi's GranFondo
race.tagline: King Ridge through the redwoods. 8,500 feet of Sonoma County climbing. Wine country meets Pacific Coast. Northern California's signature gran fondo.

vitals.location: Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, USA
vitals.registration: Online via levisgranfondo.com. Opens January/February. Cost: $175-225 depending on registration tier.
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple fully stocked aid stations along the route, including at King Ridge summit.
vitals.cutoff_time: Course time limit enforced. Riders must maintain reasonable pace or be swept.
route_options: Gran Fondo: 103 mi (166 km), ~8,500 ft — the full route with King Ridge
Medio Fondo: 80 mi (129 km) — skips the most remote coastal sections
Piccolo Fondo: 60 mi (97 km) — wine country focus without the coastal climbs
Growler: optional extra climbing challenge for the masochistic

climate.description: Early October in Sonoma County offers near-ideal cycling conditions. Inland valleys warm to 70-75F by midday, while the coastal segments near the Pacific run 55-65F with possible marine fog. The temperature differential between the warm, sheltered wine country valleys and the exposed coastal ridgelines can be 15-20 degrees. Occasional autumn rain, but most years are dry and clear.
challenges: Marine fog along the Pacific Coast — can be cold and damp for miles
Temperature swing between warm inland valleys and cool coastal ridgeline
Occasional gusty Pacific winds on exposed sections near the coast
Sun exposure on shadeless wine country roads in early afternoon

terrain.surface: Well-maintained paved county roads throughout. Mix of smooth wine country thoroughfares and narrower coastal routes. No gravel or unpaved sections.
features: King Ridge — 4+ miles of sustained climbing through coastal redwood forest, the signature effort
Hauser Bridge climb — punchy ascent on narrow roads
Coleman Valley Road — rolling climb with Pacific views before the coastal descent
Descents through redwoods require attention — narrow, shaded, occasionally damp

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 64. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 63.

course_description.character: A greatest-hits reel of Sonoma County cycling compressed into 103 miles. The route oscillates between three distinct landscapes — sun-drenched wine country, dark redwood forest canyons, and exposed Pacific Coast ridgeline — with enough climbing to keep the legs honest throughout. The climbing is punchy and repeated rather than sustained Alpine grinding. King Ridge is the centerpiece: a 4-mile effort through cathedral redwoods that defines the entire ride.
course_description.signature_challenge: King Ridge through the coastal redwoods. The climb itself is honest — 4+ miles at 5-9% — but the setting elevates it beyond mere physical effort. The transition from dark forest to sunlit Pacific ridgeline is one of American cycling's great moments.

logistics.airport: San Francisco (SFO) — 1.5 hours. Oakland (OAK) — 1.5 hours. Santa Rosa (STS) — 15 minutes (limited flights, mostly Alaska Airlines).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Santa Rosa for proximity to the start/finish. Hotels run $150-300/night on event weekend. Healdsburg (20 min north) and Petaluma (30 min south) are alternatives. Book early — wine country weekends fill fast. Airbnb options in the surrounding countryside can be excellent value.
logistics.food: Sonoma County is one of America's great food regions. Carb loading is not a problem. Post-ride, the wine country dining scene rewards well — just pace yourself on the Pinot Noir the night before.

history.origin_story: Created by Levi Leipheimer, a former Tour de France top-10 finisher and Sonoma County resident who wanted to share his training roads with the cycling public. The route was designed around Leipheimer's favorite local rides, anchored by King Ridge — the climb he considered the crown jewel of Sonoma County cycling. The event grew rapidly to become one of the largest and most respected gran fondos in the US, drawing 4,000-5,000 riders annually.
notable_moments: 2009: Inaugural edition — Levi designs the route from his personal training roads
King Ridge becomes the signature climb of American gran fondo cycling
Multiple distance options (Piccolo, Medio, Gran Fondo, Growler) broaden accessibility
Consistently ranks among the top US gran fondos by cycling media
history.reputation: The gold standard for US gran fondos. King Ridge through the redwoods is the single best climb in American sportive cycling.

biased_opinion.summary: Levi's GranFondo is the best gran fondo in the western US, and it's not particularly close. The King Ridge climb through coastal redwoods is the reason — a 4-mile sustained effort through cathedral groves that opens onto Pacific Coast views, giving you the kind of ride-defining moment that most US sportives simply cannot manufacture. The rest of the route is a greatest-hits tour of Sonoma County: vineyard-lined roads, rolling wine country, and enough climbing to make 103 miles feel significantly harder than the distance suggests. It lacks the Alpine gravitas of European events, but what it delivers — variety, scenery, and a well-run experience — is genuinely excellent.
strengths: King Ridge through the redwoods: 4+ miles of climbing through old-growth forest, the best single climb in American gran fondo cycling
Three-landscape variety: wine country, redwood forest, Pacific Coast — all in one ride
Bay Area accessibility: 55 miles from San Francisco, multiple airports, excellent cycling infrastructure
15+ years of organizational refinement under a legitimate pro cycling pedigree
weaknesses: Lacks international prestige: a big US event, but not on the global sportive radar
Sonoma County pricing: accommodation and dining carry wine country premiums
8,500 feet of climbing is solid but won't satisfy riders chasing Alpine-scale vertical
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The best riding in Sonoma County, compressed into one October day. King Ridge alone is worth the entry fee.

final_verdict.one_liner: Levi Leipheimer's backyard roads. King Ridge through the redwoods. 103 miles of Sonoma County's finest.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want Northern California's premier cycling experience with world-class scenery and variety. Yes if King Ridge through the redwoods and Pacific Coast views sound like your kind of riding. Skip if you need Alpine-scale climbing or international competitive prestige. Skip if Sonoma County wine country pricing doesn't fit your budget.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want more climbing on the West Coast: try the Mount Tam Hill Climb or Tour of California GranFondo. If you want a destination US sportive with bigger mountains: try the Leadville Trail 100 MTB or Haute Route Asheville. If you want California coastal riding in a different format: try the Solvang Century.

youtube_data.search_query: Levi's GranFondo gran fondo cycling Santa Rosa

videos.title: Levi's Gran Fondo Race Recap | Onboard Footage
videos.description:
This is my race recap of Levi’s Gran Fondo, the Growler! It was a fun event and hopefully you enjoy the race recap of the 138 mile course with nearly 14,000ft of climbing. Please subscribe to the channel if you like these recaps and comment to let me know what you think! 

Drone and Race Footage provided by Levi’s event via Definition Films and BCC - thank you! 
https://definitionfilms.tv/
https://bccliveproductions.com/about/ 

Thumbnail Credit: Caroline Dezendorf and Adventure Scout Media
Race Info: https://www.levisgranfondo.com/
Race Results: https://my.raceresult.com/335947/ 
Strava Link: https://www.strava.com/activities/14227288156 


Where to follow me: 
Instagram:  @ paigepowered  
Website: https://www.paigepowered.com
videos.transcript:
Hello. Hello. Welcome back to the
channel. Today we are going to be
talking about Levi's Fondo. This was my
first time at the event, so I don't
didn't really know what to expect. And
historically, the Fondo has been a
longunning event that's been going on
for about 16 years. Um, it's located in
Soma County in California. Absolutely
beautiful place to ride this time of
year. And starting last year, Levi, the
race director, uh, partnered with Bike
Monkey, and they've really been trying
to rejuvenate the road scene here in the
US. Huge turnout this year. Uh, the
women, I believe, had over 65 70 elite
riders, which is a great field. And, um,
for me, I wanted to attend and support
the event mostly because Levi
implemented two major changes um, that I
think are important. And so, yeah, the
women had separate starts. We went 5
minutes after the men's race and they
also implemented a no draft rule which
was pretty awesome. Um many of you know
uh if you follow me that I personally
decided this year not to attend any
large scale events or races that don't
have separate starts. Uh it's just for
me it's not fun to race in those mixed
events anymore and uh also it's not
safe. So it's just uh yeah something I
personally don't really want to do. So
yeah, let's just jump right into the
race footage. Uh you can see it's very
chill start. The course is 138 mi long
with just under 1,400 foot of elevation
gain. For any of you that are non US
folk, uh that's 220 km and 4200 m of
vertical gain. So yeah, quite a
challenging uh course. The first 30
miles, however, is like pancake flat.
And for reference, I averaged like 110
115 watts for 30 miles. So very very
chill. At times I felt like I was on a
little recovery cafe spin with the gals
and kind of nice to chat with people.
Very different from gravel because you
can't normally sit in and soft pedal
like this. Um but yeah, eventually there
was a breakaway that formed of about
four riders and you know kind of all my
main hitters, myself included, had no
desire to chase that. We knew that the
course um throughout the entire day
would kind of take care of that um as
the day went on. So yeah, um that's kind
of how the start went. After that first
30 mi, you enter the first climbing
section which is called King's Mill. Uh
this is about an hourong section and
climb. It's a beautifully wooded area.
You've got some redwoods and there's a
lot of rolling pitches and it's about
1,000 foot of gain and 10 miles. And at
the start of that section, you can see
Cesy Decker was on the front. Sarah
Sturm was up there. And then in a pink
kit, there's also a roadie Kira who she
had just won the climbers jersey at
Redlands the week prior. So for me, I
had planned on being a bit savage this
day. Um because why not? And on the
clims, I had a simple goal. If it
pitched up, I was going to the front to
push the pace. People can't really sit
in during that time and figured, you
know, let's just uh get some suffering
and and good training in. Uh after all,
unbound is calling, they say. So yeah,
that's what I did. And um you can see in
the video some of the breakaway riders.
Uh the first one we caught it was she
was not going very fast. Um and then
eventually we did catch up to Emily
Newsome and uh Deie Strbury as well. You
know, it's it's interesting because they
did join our group at that time, but
didn't really see them after the descent
or on the next climb uh after that. So
um the main group at this point kind of
stayed consistent throughout the rest of
the day. There were a few writers
getting dropped and rejoining after the
descents. Uh but for the most part it
was myself, Lauren Stevens, um Aaliyah
from F, uh Sophia, Gwen Gibson, uh Axo,
um LDC, Cesaly, Sarah Stern, uh Alexis,
and another Sophia. I'm not even going
to try to pronounce her last name.
Um, yeah, because it's it's quite long
and I don't think US u, but I believe
this is her like rookie race season and
she had a hell of a start. Um, I know
she got initially dropped on Kings Mill,
but she caught back up and she stayed in
that league group and ended up finishing
10th. So, uh, killer killer day for her.
Um, but yeah, after Kings Mill, there's
some descending, then there's some
rolling sections, and like I said, the
descents would at times separate some
riders, uh, particularly LDC and Sturi,
but they almost always bridge back, uh,
which is which is great. And I think a
large part because our group was not
really motivated to work together in any
of the flat sections. So, it was a lot
of soft pedaling. Um, I think people
were really tired from the pace on the
climbs and in road like you can just sit
in a lot more during those those
sections, too. Um, so yeah, a lot of
yo-yoing there and and effort and pace.
Um, and just allowing that group kind of
to stay together most of the day. Um,
yeah. So then after Kings Mill, you
enter another climb called Skaggs. And
Skags was really kind of up my jam. Uh,
this was my type of climb. At the bottom
of it, you have a 20-minute climb that's
like very steady and and fun. Um, it was
about 1,500 ft and 5 miles for that
initial part, which our group did in
about 20 minutes. um don't have footage
of this, but you can take a quick glance
at the power file, see that effort was
just north of 300 watts for that that
time. And then after that study portion,
you kind of come up to these steep
rollers for a bit and then another
climbing section. So in total is about
50 minutes. Um and it was 15 miles,
which ended up being about 3,000 foot of
ascending uh during during that time.
Then after that, you descend down SKGS,
which is kind of a nice descent, not
very technical, and kind of
straightforward. So, I had planned to
kind of be towards the back of the
group, which you'll see in this footage
here. Uh, but there were definitely some
gaps that were opening up and didn't
really want to do the extra work to
close those. So, tried to make my way
up. Um, and you can also see in the
footage that the descending here was
never really like all the way down. You
kind of go up and down, up and down. And
so, thankfully during recon, I had
noticed that just because sometimes if
you do an hourong climb, you kind of
want to just check out for a little bit.
but not on this course. You have a lot
of up and down and some power uh that
still needs to be applied even in the in
the descending section. So yeah, after
Skagg's descent, you know, you're about
80 miles in and we then entered uh the
only feed where you could have outside
support. And I don't know how others
felt, but it it felt very short. And I
think our speeds coming into this were
very fast. And honestly, you never know
how feeds are going to go, if someone's
going to attack or you know what's going
to happen. So, you try to get through
them as fast as you can. And I remember
I grabbed a museette and I had two
bottles and a flow gel flask in there as
well. And then I saw the end feed sign
and I knew I was like, "Oh man, I have
to I have to dump my museette otherwise
I got to carry this with me and that
fabric's not exactly breathable." So
yeah, I kind of had a choice and just
grabbed one bottle and my my flow jaw
didn't have time to grab the other
bottle so that I could dump my museette
before the end of that feed. So that was
a bit of a bummer. Um after this
section, after the feed, there was some
footage of me and Gwen, uh and then also
Sophia. And I had several people ask
about this. Um with Gwen, I was just
simply removing her safety pin. I guess
it had come unclipped and was like
stabbing her in her back. She was like
actually bleeding. So, I just took the
safety pin out for her there. And then
with Sophia, you know, the a lot of
people were commenting on, you know,
what was going on there and kind of
criticizing her, but the reality is, um,
you know, she was complaining to the
motos and trying and, you know, I'd like
to provide some context to what she was
trying to do, but she was trying to keep
the race fair by enforcing riders to not
throw their museette bags out of the
designated feed. Um, Cesy, I don't even
know if she knew that rule. She probably
hasn't really done any road events. Um,
so she gave her feedback to a Moto which
was shown on on the broadcast and then
also Lauren uh kind of did the same
thing. She init initially stuffed it in
her jersey and then eventually gave it
to the moto. Um, you know, and that's
what Sophia was upset about. She was
kind of talking to all of us and it's a
seven plus hour event. Everyone's
carrying their trash and following the
same rules. Anyways, um yeah, for those
criticizing, you know, what that
appeared to look like, uh that's just
some context that she was trying to keep
the race, you know, somewhat fair and
enforce those rules. Yeah. So, we're
going to jump ahead now to to Geysers,
which is the last climb of the day. And
this was another hour effort, and this
climb is pretty rolly for the first 45
minutes. There's some unique gravel
sections to the climb as well. And let's
just say even the pavement sections
weren't exactly uh smooth and I would
consider those a bit rowdy too. Uh
initially I camped myself near the back
of the group knowing that the rollers
would somewhat keep things together. But
eventually on one of the descents I
found myself at the front and um yeah
turns out I can descend pretty well. So
I looked back there was a gap. Hadn't
really planned on going that early but I
just kind of felt it felt right to go.
So stepped on the gas and created a gap.
And uh shortly after, Lauren Stevens
eventually bridged back up to me, which
I thought was perfect given we still had
25 mi of headwind to get through, you
know, after that climb. And given how
poorly the group had been organizing
throughout the day, I kind of felt a
move here would likely take the wind. Um
if you could descend ahead of the group
and have the power to keep it steady to
the finish. So unfortunately, my Insta3
camera died. So from here on out, this
is basically filler footage that you'll
see. So what you hear me talking about
won't exactly line up. Um, but then also
at the same point in the race, I started
to have cramps. I didn't panic, but I
was slightly concerned. And then as the
pitches went up on Geyser, and you can
see this in my cadence file that I'll
show, there's a right-hand turn on
geysers, and then it really gets steep.
And I knew I knew during my recon, this
was by far the most important place the
whole day to have power. And I kind of
was planning on that and ready for it.
Um, but then when I stood up to respond,
because Lauren also knew that and and
applied more power here. Um, I had like
the worst hamstring cramp occur. I've
never had cramps in a race. This was
very new for me. Um, but it was so bad I
could barely move the pedals and you can
see that in a power file or the cadence
file. just like I was just trying not to
walk and it was a bit demoralizing cuz
the rest of the field passed me and kind
of you know went around me and honestly
I was like paper boying some parts
because I just couldn't I couldn't stand
um and yeah it was just not not
pleasant. So honestly I was focused on
not walking and getting to the top of
the climb because I knew the descent on
geysers would allow some reprieve and
rest. So yeah, eventually I got to the
top and at that point I think I was like
11th or yeah, 11th cuz everyone in the
group had passed me and um but yeah, I
still had like 25 miles of headwind and
I knew if I could just keep like a
steady endurance or low tempo then maybe
that would catch some people that
completely cracked. Um and yeah, that
did work. I ended up passing uh Sophia V
and Sarah Sturm and Alexis um somewhat
salvage and eighth place finish. So, not
exactly the result I would have liked,
but learned a lot of valuable lessons
and saw some glimmers of fitness and
where my potential is at, which is
always good to see. And um yeah, overall
I think the event was great. They had
live streaming which a lot of people
tuned in. Um and I think that's a really
cool progression. And I also think a lot
easier to cover road events because
there's not a lot of gravel terrain and
it's much safer for the motos to be
following across the whole day. So that
was cool to see that they um you know
were able to do that and then also
provided the athletes with some of the
footage too which we did use um and very
thankful for them uh on that as well. So
yeah, thanks for tuning in. That was
Levis's. Um hell of a day and very fun
event. Highly recommend uh if you are
able to get the chance. Um very very
challenging course for sure. So thanks
for watching. Please subscribe to the
channel and comment to keep these
rolling.
videos.curation_reason: Recent first-person race recap with detailed course breakdown, good views, and specific race experience insights.

videos.title: 2019 Levi's GranFondo - Santa Rosa, California
videos.description:
2019 Levi's GranFondo
Santa Rosa, California


Levi's GranFondo is America's Premier Gran Fondo! From the route, the service, and the place – Levi's GranFondo is America's Premier Gran Fondo. Options range from an easy 9 miles for kiddos to a mammoth 117 miles to challenge experts and professionals. Ride your bike along the edge of the continent and meet the sea. It's unlike any other.



The Growler Route / 117 mi / 10564 ft


The Growler route serves up more descents, more fir-lined canyons, more sweeping vistas, more quiet gravel, more mileage, and more climbing.


https://www.levisgranfondo.com/

videos.title: Levi's Gran Fondo 2011 - GoPro Ride Video
videos.description:
A bike ride with Levi Leipheimer. Just how much fun is Levi's Gran Fondo? See and cycle for yourself.  7,500 cyclists set out from Santa Rosa, California on October 1, 2011 in a start that strained the edges of the streets. On all routes, riders cycled through town with law enforcement handling rolling closures and intersection monitoring, ensuring safety and swift passage for riders. Out on the course, rest stops were located approximately every 12 miles with great food and beverages, mechanical support, and toilets. In between, there was full motor support and EMTs in case of emergency or the need to abandon and catch a ride for you and your bike back into town. Once the pedaling was done, all riders and their families enjoyed an excellent meal, a frosty beer, local wine, live music, kid's events, and a huge vendor exhibition back in Santa Rosa!

LEVI'S GRAN FONDO:
http://www.levisgranfondo.com

Filmed and Edited by Markus Neuert for CYCLEFILM
http://www.cyclefilm.com

FOLLOW ON TWITTER
http://www.twiter.com/cyclefilm

CONNECT ON FACEBOOK
http://www.facebook.com/cyclefilmfan

About Cyclefilm: http://cyclefilm.com
Cyclefilm is a dedicated cycling production company for cycling related DVDs, how-to cycling guides, bicycle documentaries, L'Etape du Tour, Gran Fondo recon films,  promotional videos and bicycle industry news coverage. Don't Dream it, Do it - Cyclefilm Founder/Director Markus Neuert

videos.title: Levi's Gran Fondo - Cycling in Santa Rosa, CA
videos.description:
Levi Leipheimer's King Ridge Gran Fondo cycling event in Santa Rosa on October 3rd, 2009. 
Grand Fondo 103 miles, Medio Fondo 65 miles, and Piccolo Fondo 33 miles.

quotes.text: The first 30 miles, however, is like pancake flat. And for reference, I averaged like 110 115 watts for 30 miles. So very very chill. At times I felt like I was on a little recovery cafe spin with the gals and kind of nice to chat with people.

After that first 30 mi, you enter the first climbing section which is called King's Mill. Uh this is about an hourong section and climb. It's a beautifully wooded area. You've got some redwoods and there's a lot of rolling pitches and it's about 1,000 foot of gain and 10 miles.
The course is 138 mi long with just under 1,400 foot of elevation gain. For any of you that are non US folk, uh that's 220 km and 4200 m of vertical gain. So yeah, quite a challenging uh course.

key_challenges.name: King's Mill climb
key_challenges.description: About an hour-long climbing section through beautifully wooded area with redwoods. Features a lot of rolling pitches with about 1,000 feet of gain over 10 miles. This is where the real racing begins after 30 miles of flat terrain.

terrain_notes.text: The first 30 miles are described as pancake flat, allowing riders to soft pedal and chat - very different from typical gravel racing where you can't normally sit in like this.
King's Mill section features rolling pitches through wooded terrain with redwoods, making it difficult for riders to sit in and draft when the grade pitches up.

race_day_tips.text: For the first 30 miles, expect very easy pacing - riders average around 110-115 watts during this pancake flat section, making it feel like a recovery cafe spin.
Early breakaways often get caught as the course naturally takes care of gaps throughout the day as terrain becomes more challenging.
On climbs when it pitches up, riders can't sit in and draft effectively, so this is when to push pace if you want to make selections.

additional_quotes.text: Starting last year, Levi partnered with Bike Monkey and they've really been trying to rejuvenate the road scene here in the US. Huge turnout this year with over 65-70 elite women riders.
Women had separate starts going 5 minutes after the men's race and they implemented a no draft rule which was pretty awesome. It's not fun to race in mixed events anymore and it's not safe.

rider_intel.search_text: Levi's GranFondo is a 138-mile course with just under 1,400 feet of elevation gain, held in Sonoma County, California. The race features a unique profile starting with 30 miles of pancake flat terrain where riders can soft pedal and chat, averaging around 110-115 watts. The real challenge begins at mile 30 with the King's Mill climbing section, an hour-long wooded climb through redwoods with rolling pitches gaining 1,000 feet over 10 miles. Early breakaways typically get reeled in as the course naturally handles selections. The event has been running for 16 years and recently partnered with Bike Monkey to rejuvenate US road racing, implementing separate starts for women (5 minutes after men) and a no-draft rule. The 2023 edition saw strong participation with over 65-70 elite women riders in a competitive field.
